content.full_text:
California Fair Political Practices Commission

June 7, 1991

Kevin J. LaGraff, Staff Director
California Legislature, Joint Committee on the Quincentennial
State Capitol, Room 410
Sacramento, CA 95814

Re: Your Request for Informal Assistance
Our File No. I-91-249a

Dear Mr. LaGraff:

We have previously responded to your advice request concerning the Political Reform Act's ("the Act") application to the distribution by the Joint Committee of materials regarding the Quincentennial. (LaGraff Advice Letter, I-91-249, copy enclosed.) You were informed that to the extent your query concerned conduct that had already occurred, the Commission could not provide you with assistance. (Regulation 18329(b)(8)(A), 18329(c)(4)(A), copy enclosed.) To the extent your inquiry implicated the Act's gift and disclosure provisions, you were informed that additional information was required before we could respond.

By telephone conversation on May 6, 1991, and letter May 7, 1991, you provided the additional information requested. You indicated that there were two different charts that you personally requested and thereafter received from the Sears Company, that each chart was valued at $2.50, that you received 500 copies of each in November, 1990, and that they were distributed under the auspices of Senator Mello to the members of the legislature in March and April, 1991.

With this additional information we are now able to respond to your request for advice. Because your inquiry does not

1 Government Code Sections 81000-91015. All statutory references are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated. Commission regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations section 18000, et seq. All references to regulations are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations.

reference a pending governmental decision, we provide you with informal assistance only.2

On the basis of this information, it appears that the copies of the charts were provided to you, on your request, as the staff director for the Joint Legislative Committee on the Quincentennial. Therefore, you were advised by telephone that under the Act’s gift statutes and regulations in existence at the time the materials were received (November, 1990), you are the recipient of a discloseable gift for purposes of your 1990 Statement of Economic Interest. If this information was not disclosed on your statement, an amendment to the statement would be appropriate.

The members of the legislature who thereafter received the charts would likely infer that Senator Mello was the donor. The de minimus value of the materials, however, does not require reporting and disclosure by the legislative recipients.

I hope this information adequately addresses your concerns. If you have any further questions regarding this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5901.

Sincerely,

Scott Hallabrin
Acting General Counsel

By: Jonathan S. Rothman
Counsel, Legal Division

SH:JSR:ken
Enclosures

2 Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the immunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice. (Section 83114; Regulation 18329(c).)
